# The Importance of Humanities Majors in a STEM-Driven Society

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20696%20522'%3E%3C/svg%3E "image")

Alice Dehghanzadeh

Senior Staff Writer

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20768'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

In an overwhelmingly STEM-driven society, humanities majors face constant scrutiny. The value for technological advancement is increasing ever so rapidly, often leaving the humanities in the dust. In the words of Robin Williams’ character in “Dead Poets Society,” “Medicine, law, business, [and] engineering” are all “necessary to sustain life,” while “poetry, beauty, romance, love … are what we stay alive for.”

Over winter break, a discourse suddenly emerged regarding the difference in humanities versus STEM majors’ importance and level of work on the UCSB YikYak, a popular community app. Hundreds of upvotes shone on each anonymous opinion. One user said that STEM majors “think they are superior,” while another described being non-STEM as “cringe.” Some claimed that it would be impossible for humanities majors to obtain jobs while others argued that the STEM field is oversaturated and therefore equally as difficult to find a job. It is clear that public opinion is divided, but one anonymous YikYak user captured a main point of the conversation: “You can be jobless with any degree. It’s about how you use your time in college to make connections and get internships — any major can fail at that.”

[Forbes Magazine acknowledges](https://www.forbes.com/sites/marvinkrislov/2023/11/10/why-the-humanities-matter-more-than-ever-even-when-you-keep-hearing-otherwise/) that college is expensive but that studying humanities is “every bit as important to future success in the workplace as learning to code, calculate, or raise capital.” The article goes on to explain how soft-skills, such as communication, emotional intelligence, and creativity are an essential appeal of an exceptional employee when compared to an average one. Said skills are honed by a classic liberal arts education, according to the article. Essentially, technical skills, while important, are only part of what is crucial in being an outstanding candidate.

One’s proficiency in the humanities is also a major testament to one’s ability to problem-solve and think critically, according to [The Center for Humanities and the Public Sphere](https://humanities.ufl.edu/archives/the-work-of-the-humanities-critical-thinking-in-life-and-labor/). The humanities test the limits of your mind to its greatest potential, forcing you to expand your knowledge and think beyond surface level. Through working with complex texts and historical events, students equip themselves with the necessary levels of creativity needed to work in and lead society. Decision-making is another vital asset to a humanities student, as it appropriately challenges them to look at several points of view and make informed judgments.

Ultimately, a humanities education aids students in becoming members of society that are well-versed in problem-solving.

The interconnection between STEM and humanities ideas is what is most challenging to achieve yet also most rewarding. In an ever-changing society, we need capable thinkers of both worlds to bridge the gap and work together holistically to produce innovative ideas.

Many of the world’s problems — inequality, discrimination, climate change, and political division, to name a few — are rooted in human behavior and ethics. To the humanities student, these issues provide the lens through which one can understand and analyze the intricacies of human beliefs and values. I believe that studying philosophy, literature, history, and other fields help humanities students comprehend societal challenges, because they provide the context to understanding the motivations and consequences of past events. With this knowledge, humanities students are better furnished with the ability to enact positive change. Humanities majors are not solely important in college but beyond, aiding in the foundation of a thoughtful and compassionate world.

STEM also plays an essential role in founding a modern society. The advancements in science and technology help drive society, and directly impact how we live. This progress cannot exist, however, without a comprehensive understanding of how the world works, which is where humanities come in, giving STEM the ethics and values to which they must learn to work with.

Both STEM and humanities fields remain crucial in providing students with the skills to live as thought-provoking and meaningful citizens of society. Instead of being polarized, students should come together to celebrate their differences in the fields they choose to study while acknowledging that one cannot live without the other. The potential for collaborative power is indefinite.

# Challenging educational perceptions: Why humanities matter just as much as STEM

![Challenging educational perceptions: Why humanities matter just as much as STEM](https://i0.wp.com/quchronicle.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/humanities-stem.jpg?fit=1200%2C779&ssl=1)

If you asked yourself what some of the most important careers are, what comes to mind? Doctors, surgeons, nurses, scientists and engineers may be at the top of that list. But why not teachers, writers, authors, journalists or artists?

As someone who has always been terrible at math and science, careers in STEM (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) were never really an option for me. But as I grew up, the only professions I ever heard about were the ones that required years of medical school.

While jobs in STEM are important, I feel as though the humanities are severely underrated and underrepresented. Without English, nobody would know how to read, write or communicate, three essential skills needed to perform those jobs in STEM.

Fewer students are earning degrees in humanities disciplines, even though employment and earnings are increasing, according to the [U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics](https://www.bls.gov/careeroutlook/2007/winter/art01.pdf).

Even though I hate math and science, I can understand the importance of taking classes under these subjects. STEM students should feel the same about their required humanities classes.

I believe that Quinnipiac’s University Curriculum requirements are beneficial, because they ensure that students are exploring all areas of interest and not just the ones that pertain to their major.

Subjects that fall under humanities, such as literature, history, philosophy, anthropology, geography and sociology “refine and reassure career perspectives alongside an in-depth understanding of the world in historical, creative and philosophical senses,” according to [Quinnipiac University](https://www.qu.edu/academics/about-our-programs/areas-of-interest/humanities-culture/).

These subjects teach us imperative skills like critical thinking, communication, questioning and reasoning. With these abilities, we are able to form strong opinions, defend ideas and arguments and foster creativity.

However, these disciplines seem to be falling through the cracks due to issues with credibility.

The “issue of credibility” means that people think humanities are overly opinionated and political, leading to a lack of public trust, per [The Washington Post](https://www.washingtonpost.com/outlook/2022/04/15/humanities-sciences-credibility-crisis-public-trust/).

“The public doesn’t seem to trust that we are engaging in real, methodical scholarly inquiry — or, at least, that such inquiries amount to much more than informed or pretentious opinion-making.” Over half of Americans believe that “humanities attract people who are somewhat pretentious and elitist,” according to a survey taken by the [American Academy of Arts and Science](https://www.amacad.org/sites/default/files/publication/downloads/The-Humanities-in-American-Life.pdf).

I think that couldn’t be farther from the truth.

As someone who has studied humanities my whole life, I believe that they encourage people to think outside the box and to build their own ideas. The humanities push people to stay in touch with reality and the issues of the current world, which may be more necessary than ever.

I didn’t know what I wanted to pursue until my sophomore year of high school, when I took a class on journalism. I instantly fell in love with print journalism and writing about current events.

With the strong push and advocacy for STEM professions, I didn’t even know what journalism was up until that point.

Also, the fact that the outcome of studying disciplines in humanities are not easily measurable can often drive students away. This causes difficulties when demonstrating the impact that liberal arts has.

With society’s heavy emphasis on economic prosperity and productivity, choosing what makes you happy over what’s logical can be a difficult decision. The priority is wealth-oriented, and humanities careers may not seem like they can get you there.

Funding for education tends to be allocated based on perceived economic benefits. As a result of this, humanities programs are underinvested in compared to STEM, according to [The New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2016/02/22/business/a-rising-call-to-promote-stem-education-and-cut-liberal-arts-funding.html).

On top of this, cultural trends and attitudes shape perceptions of these subjects. If success is tied to the STEM field, that is generally where students will flock, causing humanities to be overshadowed.

Living in an era of constant technological advancement can also put a strain on majoring in a humanities discipline. If science and technology are seen as more critical for the improvement of society, humanistic inquiry is overlooked.

Liberal arts are often deemed inferior and something that people who aren’t smart enough for STEM go to as a last resort. It’s never seen as a genuine interest someone might have.

I always wonder why people ask me what I plan to do with my bachelor’s degree in journalism. Due to the perspective that liberal arts degrees bring a lack of job opportunities, some parents shame their children for their desire to pursue disciplines in humanities. While the jobs may not pay as much compared to STEM, they are still purposeful and significant.

# Measuring the impact of humanities on STEM-focused education

#### MIT Integrated Learning Initiative grantees research how STEM and humanities complement each other.

*By MITili*

Wiebke Denecke (魏樸和) is professor of East Asian Literatures. She was trained in sinology, Japanology, Korean studies, philosophy, and medicine in her native Germany, in Hungary, Norway, Dalian, Taipei, Tokyo, Seoul, and Boston. She received her BA and MA from the University of Göttingen and her PhD from Harvard University. Her research and teaching encompass the classical literatures and philosophical traditions of China, Japan, and Korea, comparative studies of East Asia and the premodern world, world literature, and the politics of cultural heritage and memory.

Tristan G. Brown is a historian of late imperial (“early modern”) China. His research focuses on the ways in which law, science, environment, and religion interacted in China from the sixteenth to nineteenth centuries. His first book, “[Laws of the Land: Fengshui and the State in Qing Dynasty China](https://press.princeton.edu/books/hardcover/9780691246734/laws-of-the-land)” (Princeton University Press, 2023), examines Qing (1644–1912) judicial archives to investigate the uses of cosmology in Chinese law during an era of great economic and environmental change. He is preparing a second book that employs Chinese, Arabic, and Persian sources to examine the history of Islam in the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644), when many Muslim communities integrated into Chinese society.

Denecke and Brown are recent recipients of a grant from the MIT Integrated Learning Initiative (MITili) — part of MIT Open Learning — to research educational effectiveness. Their project, “[Measuring the Impact of Humanities Learning in an Age of STEM](https://mitili.mit.edu/research/measuring-impact-humanities-learning-age-stem)”, looks to examine the reason humanities studies are in decline and what impact humanities studies may have on a STEM focused education.

#### **Your studies and work span an impressive global array of countries and regions, how does that help you in being an effective educator?**

D: There is the obvious. If you studied and worked in many different places, you lived in alternative realities and can teach from multiple perspectives, in terms of local knowledge, cultural appreciation, and even teaching methodology — which varies widely across the world. Our “active learning” style is certainly news to instructor-centered East Asian education systems! Then there is the more subtle, in the classroom. When you teach students with family heritage or international students from the regions where you lived it makes a world of difference to know the languages, values, places & lore. It makes these students feel “at home” and even the other students in the class pick up on that vibe of familiarity and get hungry for global experiences in their studies and work — we want that! And then there is the less obvious effect on my teaching, something I want to sensitize students to. “Humanities” appear in many different shapes in different parts of the world, but most of the debates regarding humanities education and the proverbial “humanities crisis” and budget cuts are driven by the situation in the US or Europe. Too little students know that there is a “humanities boom” in other parts of the world, most notably mainland China, where classical (especially Confucian) studies are promoted as the official party line and new programs that offer the study of “Chinese Classics” in competitive juxtaposition with the Western canon have recently emerged. It’s an investment in ideological nation branding of a certain kind. I always paint the varied picture of the humanities across the world for my students and urge them to stand up for their embattled humanities education. If they realize that our “crisis humanities” in Western democracies could be outvoiced someday by “humanities booms” elsewhere that are mobilized for divisive nation branding, they are more likely to care about that crisis and speak out.

B: It is remarkable how many of my students tell me that they never have had the chance to take a course on Chinese history before. Some of them have heard about the Three Kingdoms era (220–280 CE) or the Great Wall — but that’s often about it. It’s exhilarating to introduce MIT students to the calligraphy of Su Shi (1037–1101 CE) and the paintings of Zhao Mengfu (1254–1322 CE) for the first time. But perhaps due to my background in Arabic and Islamic studies, I’m also always thinking about what was happening in other parts of the world at the same time. Balancing a plethora of views from the local to the global allows for some dramatic cross-cultural reveals over the course of the semester: the appearance of pseudo-Phagspa script (the official writing system of the Mongol Yuan Empire) in late medieval Western Art or Arabic and Persian inscriptions rendered onto Ming (1368–1644) porcelain. Those are the moments that help make an undergraduate education.

#### **In your research work with MITili, you’ll be measuring the impact of the humanities on learning during a time when so many are focusing on technology and in particular AI. How important is it, in your mind, to maintain a balance in the curriculum these days?**

D: Yes, indeed. The “AI boom” or also “AI doom boom” makes a great mass of smart people exercise their minds on current technological developments, applications, and futuristic scenarios. This is all very important and we’ll see what emerges when the dust settles. We do want to engage students as early and honestly as possible about their uses of AI. In the new fifth edition of the *Norton Anthology of World Literature*, which I am co-editing, we included an AI-generated translation of the German poet Rainer Maria Rilke’s poignantly personal “Archaic Torso of Apollo,” alongside with “human” English translations in a “Translation Lab” feature for comparison. It’s not that bad actually!

AI has certainly huge implications for the mass-administered humanities-class essays. That might make oral exams, and more unique and less template-applicable syllabi and essay designs more popular in the future. MIT has an unusually strong humanities requirement and I believe it should continue to go strong on things that hardly require any technology but are central to the business of the humanities: practicing critical thinking, highlighting human agency and responsibility for the world we are shaping, savoring the complexity of values and human societies past and present, being touched by a good line of poetry…and how all of this will actively blend with their STEM education and their work in our STEM-driven world.

B: Echoing what Wiebke says here, I feel that, in many ways, AI is a welcome challenge for humanities education. Essay-based evaluations for humanities have become increasingly common over the past few decades, which makes the arrival of ChatGPT disconcerting in many ways. But students cannot use ChatGPT on in-class examinations, which may see a renaissance of sorts in the years to come. I offer classes that employ both essays and exams, and I have noticed that putting a midterm on a course syllabus tends *not* to scare MIT students away. In fact, many of them like to know where they precisely stand grade-wise in the middle of the term. For instances where essays are assigned, MIT’s humanities faculty are also finding ways to integrate AI technologies — for instance, by permitting its limited use if cited properly. Our departments are clearly places to watch to see the implementations of these new experimental kinds of pedagogy.

#### **Most people don’t associate MIT with the humanities and art. What “hidden secrets” would you like our audience to know about the breadth of humanities and art available at MIT?**

D: There are many and I wouldn’t want to pick. Instead, I want to emphasize the one blatant secret that is underappreciated when the humanities are typically perceived as marginalized at MIT. Students can breathe and thrive in our subjects because our curriculum is less focused on their professional interests. They often talk about how it helps them “shift gears” both mentally and emotionally. What we want to make sure, though, is to not train students with a “split brain,” where their STEM work lives separately from the kind of critical ethical and sociopolitical reasoning or emotional maturity, they are honing in their humanities courses.

B: Two things come to mind. I think one “hidden secret” is that humanities classes at MIT serve a very practical function in an undergraduate’s education: humanities instructors often get to know their students on a deeper level than those of other kinds of classes. Of course, there are always exceptions. But in talking to colleagues, I’ve often wondered why so many undergraduates ask for letters of recommendation for a class on environmental history or East Asian literature when they’re applying to medical school or a finance internship. I gradually realized that many students felt we had something meaningful to say about their development here at the Institute.

The second secret I think lies is a recognition — which many students may only develop with time — of the level of accomplishment of MIT’s humanities faculty. My colleagues take students on trips overseas during IAP, testify before Congress, pen editorials on current events, and write multiple books. Students know MIT humanities faculty are “smart,” but they often don’t know what we do besides teach their classes. I encourage them to ask!

#### **What do you hope your students gain from having meaningful dialogue with other students who are engaging in humanities studies?**

D: Eavesdropping on students who walk in or out of the classroom discussing their take on the topics of the day is a pleasure for me as an educator. I overheard a Chinese heritage student say to her friend: “You know what’s truly helpful about the class: we read the Confucian *Analects*, whose values are still more or less engrained in East Asian societies, and I can finally “get” and psychoanalyze my parents.” I do what I can to get students talking to each other and have them take their conversations outside the classroom. Last spring we did a “Global Classroom” experience where students in Seoul collaborated with MIT students on writing a civil service examination essay on an 18th century exam question about the *Analects.* Passing that exam got you a job in the extensive royal bureaucracy and was thus crucial to social success back then.My students were amazed to hear debates about how the Confucian tradition is alive and challenged in their Korean partners’ family lives — a controversial topic across the generations in contemporary Korea. With their peers, students venture into much more personal cross-application to their own lives and experience how other students create yet other forms of meaning. “Humanities studies” are academic subjects, and overly personal “opinions” are frowned upon. But students should be inspired to make the rich store of issues, stories, divergent perspectives and ambiguities they encounter in class their very own. That trickle-down-effect of students’ cross-application of their humanities education to their own lives and in particular to how it matters to them as STEM majors is one thing we want to measure. We are not just interested in “tech-ed-up” humanities subjects that have direct applicability to STEM subjects. But in the kind of cross-applied “learning for life” that will make our graduates into responsible, culturally and globally sensitive citizens, communicators, and mediators. How is it happening? — Is it happening? How can we enhance it? We want to look into this.

B: To expand on what Wiebke has said, I would say that course reputations spread by word of mouth. When I ask students why they enrolled in one of my classes, they often reply that they heard about it from a roommate or a friend. I’m thrilled to hear this response, because it means they had a conversation outside of class about Chinese history. I often tell my students in the fall that their humanities classes are the ones that they’ll go home and talk to their families about over the winter holiday. The classes we teach, the conversation we foster, and the lessons we offer are the ones that never get old. Accordingly, I think the humanities have a potentially big role to play in alumni relations — that is, helping alumni maintain ties to the Institute long after they have graduated.

#### **What is your favorite thing about being a part of MIT?**

D: The students, the colleagues, the conversations, infinite … the dome!

B: I agree with Wiebke and I especially cherish my incredibly supportive colleagues. I would also add that no MIT student has ever told me that a class discussion or assignment was ever too difficult or too complex. They’re there for the challenge, which means I’m never bored.

*Originally published at* [*https://mitili.mit.edu/*](https://mitili.mit.edu/news/qa-geoffrey-kocks-phd-candidate-mit-economics-department)*.*

Combining STEM and Humanities: Broaden skills and enrich learning
=================================================================

[January 10, 2025](https://citl.news.niu.edu/2025/01/10/combining-stem-and-humanities-broaden-skills-and-enrich-learning/ "9:35 am")[Amanda Hirsch](https://citl.news.niu.edu/author/a1404391/ "View all posts by Amanda Hirsch")[Resources](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/resources/), [Teaching](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/teaching/), [Tips](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/tips/)

STEM majors might kick off their careers with higher salaries, but research shows that humanities majors often catch up by mid-career, thanks to their adaptability and critical thinking skills—qualities that industries are valuing more and more these days (Goldfine, 2024). This highlights the growing need for interdisciplinary approaches to tackle complex problems that require creative, out-of-the-box solutions. Bringing STEM and the humanities together doesn’t just expand students’ skill sets—it also makes learning more enriching and prepares them for lasting success, both professionally and personally.

The Value of Interdisciplinary Learning
=======================================

![Image 3: Left right human brain concept. Creative art brain side (right) explodes with paint splatter; and mathematical brain side (left) with formulas.](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-2018098760-300x169.jpg)Student complaints about non-STEM general education courses are both misguided and short-sighted—STEM and humanities aren’t opposites; they actually complement each other (Bourdeau & Wood, 2019). One promising solution is “Humanistic STEM,” which blends scientific and technical studies with an emphasis on human welfare, values, and culture (Bourdeau & Wood, 2019; Bourdeau & Wood, 2021). This approach meets the growing demand for STEM graduates who are not only technically skilled but also creative, empathetic, ethical, and globally aware. Unlike STEAM, which typically emphasizes the arts, Humanistic STEM (H-STEM) prioritizes interdisciplinary collaboration and integration of perspectives across STEM and humanities (Bourdeau & Wood, 2021). Humanities courses encourage students to engage with nuanced issues and explore diverse perspectives, providing transformative learning experiences (MIT Open Learning, 2024). By studying both humanities and STEM, students can become well-rounded and globally aware graduates who think criticallyare ready to solve tomorrow’s toughest challenges with both technical expertise and ethical insight.

Humanities Complements STEM
===========================

Studying subjects like literature, history, and philosophy helps students build skills—like empathy, creativity, critical thinking, communication, and adaptability—that they will use throughout their careers (Goldfine, 2024; Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2019). Abilities such as writing, oral communication, problem-solving, teamwork, ethical decision-making, sociopolitical reasoning, and leadership are critical for success and advancement in today’s ever-changing workplaces (Goldfine, 2024; Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2019; MIT Open Learning, 2024). Humanities studies also spark curiosity, empathy, and global sensibility, while encouraging self-expression and helping students to feel connected to the broader world (Goldfine, 2024). They push students to think critically about abstract ideas and approach challenges from fresh perspectives, boosting their ability to solve complex issues in creative and innovative ways (Goldfine, 2024).

![Image 4: Woman standing at podium with laptop giving a speech or presentation. ](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1348871022-300x191.jpg)Storytelling, for example, plays an important role in making technical work impactful. Whether crafting compelling grant proposals and journal articles, or communicating ideas in public and professional settings, being able to frame technical work within broader societal narratives is the key to advancing STEM research (Koblitz, 2017). Humanities disciplines like literature, history, and world languages can help STEM professionals develop the skill of storytelling, enabling them to link their work to the human experience and drive meaningful change. Humanities also contribute to STEM in practical ways, such as:

*   Writing clear and persuasive scholarly articles or funding proposals
*   Navigating ethical minefields in technology and AI
*   Enhancing teamwork and leadership through empathy and effective communication

When STEM and humanities come together, students gain versatile skills that strengthen technical abilities while preparing them to approach challenges holistically and through a socially conscious lens.

STEM also Strengthens Humanities
================================

![Image 5: Sculpture Thinker With Golden VR Glasses On Purple Background](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1204290480-300x191.jpg)Humanities doesn’t just benefit STEM—the relationship works both ways. Bringing STEM into humanities studies allows students to blend technical skills with critical and creative thinking. For example, data analysis and visualization can help students dive deeper into historical trends, linguistic patterns, or cultural shifts. STEM tools also open the door to exciting digital projects like 3D modeling of historical artifacts, virtual reality experiences for cultural studies, or AI-assisted translation. These tools can even enhance storytelling with interactive timelines or digital narratives. Additionally, STEM can introduce humanities students to ethical dilemmas in technology, such as artificial intelligence and climate change, equipping them for interdisciplinary career opportunities where technical and ethical insights go hand in hand.

Challenges and Misconceptions
=============================

Some common misconceptions about integrating STEM and humanities include beliefs that “Humanities aren’t relevant to technical careers” and “STEM and humanities require separate skill sets.” These beliefs overlook how essential humanities are for fostering critical skills that STEM fields rely on, like communication, ethical reasoning, and creative problem-solving. While addressing these misconceptions is a key step, the bigger challenge lies in the logistics of creating and sustaining interdisciplinary programs.

![Image 6: Close-up of team of people brainstorming while analyzing mind map laid out on a table.](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1358014160-300x200.jpg)Building successful STEM-humanities programs requires collaboration between faculty, administrators, and other stakeholders. For example, team-teaching logistics require careful coordination to balance workloads and ensure meaningful engagement between disciplines. Developing flexible, innovative curricula also demands ongoing creativity and adaptability from educators. These efforts are only sustainable with continual budgetary support and institutional commitment to provide the resources and incentives needed for cross-disciplinary collaboration (Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2021).

Challenges like budget constraints, rigid or insular departmental structures, and differing visions among faculty can hinder progress. However, potential solutions like encouraging team teaching to share the workload and foster cross-disciplinary collaboration, and creating flexible curricula to integrate STEM and humanities concepts can make a big difference. Ultimately, institutions need to foster a culture of innovation that values interdisciplinary programs and recognizes their long-term value in preparing students for a rapidly changing and increasingly interconnected world.

The Future of Humanistic STEM
=============================

![Image 7: Hologram of the Brain and Statue of Goddess Themis. ](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-2149868673-300x165.jpg)The future of Humanistic STEM lies in its potential to address global challenges, such as the rise of AI, climate change, and public health crises, by combining the critical and ethical insights of the humanities with the technical expertise of STEM. As technology continues to reshape industries and society, interdisciplinary education equips students with adaptability, communication, and critical thinking skills, essential qualities in an ever-shifting professional landscape. Investing in humanities and STEM together not only enhances students’ career prospects but also fosters personal growth, enabling students to connect their work to broader societal narratives and make meaningful contributions. Educators and institutions should champion interdisciplinary approaches to prepare students for the complex challenges of today and tomorrow. As faculty, you can start by exploring and advocating for interdisciplinary programs to embrace the intersection of STEM and humanities in your own discipline.

SUMMARY:
The Death of Humanities Education: Why We Need to Revalue the Arts and Sciences | by Sahin Ahmed, Data Scientist | Medium By fostering a critical and questioning mindset, humanities education can empower students to become vocal critics of government actions and policies, leading to social unrest and political challenges. The decline in humanities education in favor of STEM fields reflects broader economic and societal shifts, yet the humanities remain crucial for fostering critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and deep understanding of human conditions. As technological and ethical challenges increase, revaluing humanities education is essential for developing well-rounded citizens capable of tackling complex issues with wisdom and integrity, thereby ensuring a balanced educational approach that values both arts and sciences equally.

FULL CONTENT:

> **_“The death of humanities education is not just a crisis in academia — it’s a crisis for our society. As we race towards a future dominated by technology, we risk losing the very disciplines that teach us what it means to be human.”_**

In today’s rapidly advancing society, the value of humanities education is under siege. Subjects like philosophy, literature, and history are increasingly viewed as impractical and outdated compared to the seemingly boundless opportunities in STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics) fields. This shift in perception has led to a dramatic decline in the pursuit of humanities degrees, influenced by economic pressures and a market-driven approach to education.

However, it is crucial to revalue humanities education not only to preserve our cultural and intellectual heritage but also to ensure a balanced approach to technological and societal progress. Humanities disciplines foster critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and a deeper understanding of the human condition—all essential for navigating the complexities of modern life.

The Importance of Humanities
============================

Critical Thinking and Analysis
------------------------------

The humanities play a crucial role in fostering critical thinking and encouraging individuals to question societal norms. By engaging with diverse perspectives and exploring complex ideas, students of the humanities develop the ability to analyze and critique the world around them. This skill set is essential for identifying and challenging injustices, fostering innovation, and driving societal progress.

Influential thinkers in the Indian context, such as Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, exemplify the impact of the humanities. Ambedkar, a scholar of law, economics, and political science, utilized his education to challenge the caste system and advocate for social justice. His critical examination of social hierarchies and his efforts to draft the Indian Constitution have had a lasting impact on Indian society. Ambedkar’s work demonstrates how a strong foundation in the humanities can empower individuals to drive significant societal change.

Ethical and Social Awareness
----------------------------

The humanities contribute significantly to understanding human conditions and navigating ethical dilemmas. Subjects like philosophy, literature, and history provide insights into human experiences, cultural contexts, and moral questions, fostering empathy and ethical reasoning. This awareness is vital in a rapidly advancing technological world, where decisions have far-reaching implications for society.

In India, the development and implementation of new technologies, such as Aadhaar (the world’s largest biometric identification system), illustrate the importance of ethical oversight. While Aadhaar has streamlined many administrative processes and enhanced service delivery, it has also raised significant ethical concerns regarding privacy, data security, and potential misuse. The humanities offer a framework to critically assess these technologies, ensuring that their implementation respects individual rights and promotes social welfare.

For instance, literature and media studies can shed light on how Aadhaar impacts various segments of society, particularly marginalized communities. Philosophical discussions on privacy and human rights can guide policymakers in balancing technological benefits with ethical considerations. Without this interdisciplinary approach, technological advancements risk exacerbating inequalities and infringing on fundamental rights.

> The humanities remind us that technological innovation must be guided by a deep understanding of human values and ethical principles. By integrating critical thinking and ethical reasoning into the development and implementation of new technologies, we can ensure that progress serves the broader good of humanity and promotes a just and equitable society.

The Decline of Humanities Education
===================================

**Historical Context**
----------------------

Throughout history, the liberal arts have held a significant place in the development of society. Rooted in the classical education systems of ancient Greece and Rome, subjects like philosophy, literature, and rhetoric were seen as essential for cultivating informed and virtuous citizens. This foundation persisted through the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, where a well-rounded education in the liberal arts was considered vital for personal and intellectual growth.

However, the Enlightenment and the Industrial Revolution brought about profound changes to education. The Enlightenment emphasized reason, science, and empirical evidence, leading to significant advancements in various fields of study. The Industrial Revolution further shifted the focus towards practical and technological skills necessary for industrial and economic growth. These periods marked the beginning of a gradual shift away from the humanities towards a more utilitarian approach to education.

**Modern Perception**
---------------------

In the modern era, this shift has become even more pronounced. STEM fields are now viewed as more practical and valuable, given their direct contributions to technological innovation and economic development. The demand for computer scientists, engineers, and healthcare professionals has surged, overshadowing the perceived utility of humanities disciplines.

Common criticisms of the humanities further contribute to their decline. Critics argue that humanities subjects are elitist, focusing on abstract and overanalyzed concepts like race and gender without producing tangible, marketable skills. They contend that humanities graduates face limited job prospects and struggle to pay off student debt, making these fields seem impractical and outdated in a world that prioritizes immediate economic returns and technological progress. This perception is reinforced by the high cost of college education, which discourages students from pursuing degrees in fields that are not seen as financially rewarding.

As a result, the humanities are increasingly marginalized in educational institutions and society at large, leading to a significant decline in their influence and presence.

The Economic Argument
=====================

**High Cost of Education**
--------------------------

The financial burden of college education is a significant factor in the decline of humanities degrees. As tuition fees continue to rise, students and their families are increasingly concerned about the return on investment that a college degree offers. The cost of higher education has skyrocketed over the past few decades, leading to substantial student debt upon graduation. In this economic climate, students are often dissuaded from pursuing humanities degrees, which are perceived to have lower earning potential compared to STEM fields.

When faced with the prospect of substantial debt, many students opt for degrees that promise higher salaries and job security. The humanities, often viewed as less directly tied to lucrative career paths, suffer as a result. The perception that a degree in philosophy, literature, or gender studies will not provide sufficient financial returns to justify the cost of education steers students towards more “practical” fields. This financial calculus undermines the value of the humanities, reducing their appeal despite their broader intellectual and societal benefits.

The Role of Universities
========================

**Market-Driven Education**
---------------------------

> _“Universities have increasingly adopted a market-driven approach to education, prioritizing profit and the production of marketable skills. This shift is influenced by the pressures of capitalism, which demands that educational institutions operate efficiently and contribute to the economy by producing a workforce that meets market needs._ As a result, universities emphasize programs that align with industry demands, such as engineering, computer science, and business, while reducing support for humanities departments.”

This focus on marketable skills impacts educational choices and opportunities, steering resources and attention away from the humanities. Universities invest in STEM facilities, research, and faculty, while humanities programs face budget cuts, reduced course offerings, and lower enrollment. The prioritization of profit and immediate economic returns shapes curricula and institutional policies, reinforcing the idea that only degrees with clear market value are worth pursuing.

The impact of capitalism on education thus narrows the scope of what is considered valuable knowledge. It marginalizes fields that do not directly contribute to economic productivity, despite their critical role in fostering critical thinking, cultural awareness, and ethical reasoning. This market-driven mindset limits students’ educational opportunities and undermines the holistic development that a balanced education can provide.

The trend towards hyper-specialization
--------------------------------------

This has also contributed to the decline of the humanities. While specialization can lead to advancements in specific fields, it often comes at the expense of a holistic education. Rarely are interdisciplinary approaches encouraged, which are vital for addressing complex societal issues that require insights from both the sciences and the humanities.

For example, understanding the ethical implications of new technologies like artificial intelligence requires input from computer science, philosophy, sociology, and law. By fostering interdisciplinary studies, universities can ensure that students are equipped with a broad range of skills and perspectives necessary for navigating the complexities of the modern world.

**Resistance to Political Activism**
------------------------------------

Universities have historically been hotbeds of political activism and social change. In many cases, student movements have played a crucial role in challenging government policies and advocating for democratic rights. However, in an effort to maintain control and prevent dissent, universities may discourage political activism, particularly within the humanities, which are often at the forefront of such movements.

> “By discouraging political activism and critical engagement with societal issues, universities risk becoming complacent institutions that prioritize economic outcomes over the development of informed and active citizens.”

Government Reluctance to Promote Humanities Education
=====================================================

**Fear of Student Protests**
----------------------------

Governments around the world, including in India, are often reluctant to promote humanities education due to the critical and questioning nature it fosters among students. Humanities disciplines encourage students to analyze societal structures, question authority, and advocate for social justice, which can lead to increased activism and protests.

> “Historically, student protests have been a powerful force for change. In India, the role of student movements in political and social change is well-documented. The anti-Emergency protests in the 1970s and more recent movements such as those led by students at Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU) highlight how humanities-educated students often lead the charge in challenging government policies and advocating for democratic rights.”

These movements can be seen as threatening by those in power, as they often challenge the status quo and demand accountability and transparency from the government. By fostering a critical and questioning mindset, humanities education can empower students to become vocal critics of government actions and policies, leading to social unrest and political challenges.

**Control and Conformity**
--------------------------

**To maintain control and prevent dissent, governments may prioritize STEM education, which is often viewed as less politically charged and more aligned with economic growth and technological advancement.** By steering students towards fields that are perceived as more “neutral” and economically beneficial, governments can reduce the likelihood of large-scale student movements and maintain a more controlled and compliant populace.

Additionally, funding for humanities programs is often limited, with more resources directed towards fields that are deemed critical for national development and competitiveness. This creates an environment where humanities education is undervalued and less accessible, further discouraging students from pursuing these disciplines.

Revaluing Humanities Education
==============================

**Policy Recommendations**
--------------------------

To revalue and support humanities education, several policy measures can be implemented at both governmental and institutional levels:

**Increase Funding for Humanities Departments:**

Governments and educational institutions should increase funding for humanities departments. This includes investments in faculty positions, research grants, and scholarships specifically for humanities students. Increased funding can help enhance the quality and breadth of humanities programs, making them more attractive to prospective students.

**Develop Interdisciplinary Programs:**

Universities should develop and promote interdisciplinary programs that integrate the humanities with science and technology fields. These programs can demonstrate the relevance of humanities skills in diverse sectors, highlighting how critical thinking and ethical considerations are essential in tech-driven industries.

**Incentivize Enrollment in Humanities:**

Offering tuition waivers, scholarships, and financial incentives for students choosing humanities majors can help increase enrollment. These incentives could be tied to commitments to work in specific sectors that benefit from humanities skills, such as education, public service, or non-profits.

**Curriculum Development:**

Reform curricula to include more humanities content in all fields of study. For example, engineering and business programs could include courses in ethics, history, and communication as part of their core requirements, emphasizing the value of a well-rounded education.

**Societal Shifts**
-------------------

Changing societal perceptions of the humanities is crucial for their revaluation. This involves cultural shifts and enhanced public awareness about the benefits of humanities education:

**Public Awareness Campaigns:**

Governments and educational institutions should run public awareness campaigns that highlight successful individuals in various industries who have benefited from humanities education. These campaigns can also showcase the role of humanities in fostering critical thinking and ethical decision-making.

**Media Partnerships:**

Partnering with media outlets to feature stories, documentaries, and discussions on the importance of humanities can help change public perception. These stories can focus on how humanities graduates contribute to society and solve real-world problems.

**Encourage Corporate Endorsements:**

Corporations can be encouraged to endorse the value of humanities skills in the workplace. Having industry leaders speak about the importance of skills like critical thinking, ethical judgment, and effective communication can help elevate the status of the humanities.

**Balanced Education Advocacy:**

Advocates for education reform should emphasize the importance of a balanced education that equally values arts and sciences. This can involve lobbying for educational policies that integrate humanities perspectives into the STEM-focused educational system.

**Community Engagement:**

Universities and colleges should encourage humanities departments to engage with local communities through public lectures, free courses, and community service projects. This engagement can demonstrate the practical value and relevance of humanities education in addressing community issues.

By implementing these policy recommendations and promoting societal shifts, the value of humanities education can be reestablished. This would not only benefit individuals in terms of personal growth and career flexibility but also enrich society by producing well-rounded citizens capable of thoughtful leadership and innovation.

Conclusion
==========

The decline in humanities education in favor of STEM fields reflects broader economic and societal shifts, yet the humanities remain crucial for fostering critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and deep understanding of human conditions. Addressing this imbalance requires policy reforms to enhance funding and support for humanities programs, integrated interdisciplinary approaches, and societal perception shifts through awareness campaigns and community engagement. As technological and ethical challenges increase, revaluing humanities education is essential for developing well-rounded citizens capable of tackling complex issues with wisdom and integrity, thereby ensuring a balanced educational approach that values both arts and sciences equally.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Humanities education fosters critical thinking, encouraging students to engage with societal issues, advocate for justice, and become informed, active participants in their communities.
Studying humanities enhances understanding of diverse cultures, promoting empathy and appreciation for different perspectives—essential for navigating a globalized world.
Integrating humanities with STEM equips students to tackle complex global challenges, ensuring that technological advancements align with ethical considerations and societal needs.
